name: Environmental Science and Engineering

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: As a focused graduate certificate the menu of 17 courses coheres around environmental problem-solving, bridging engineering treatment (ENE 664 Physical/Chemical Treatment, ENE 665 Biological Treatment, ENE 662 Site Remediation), science (EVSC 610 Environmental Chemical Science, EVSC 612 Environmental Analysis, EVSC 616 Toxicology), and policy/impact (ENE 671 Environmental Impact Analysis, EM 631 Legal Aspects, EPS 622 Sustainable Politics). However with requirementGroups 0 and no core-vs-elective designation it reads as an eligible-course list rather than a designed pathway.
dimensions.suggestion: Designate a small required spine (e.g., one treatment, one analysis, and one policy course) so the certificate presents a defined progression instead of an undifferentiated 17-course menu.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is healthy for a certificate: missingCourseCount is 0 and deepestPrereqChain is only 1, with courses largely standalone; the two chained cases are ENE 662 (requires EM 631) and ENE 664 (requires ENE 663). CE 647 reaches back to undergraduate CE 341/341A, which is appropriate signposting rather than a dangling reference.
dimensions.suggestion: Confirm ENE 663 (the prerequisite of ENE 664) is offered on a cadence certificate students can reach, or flag ENE 664 as part of a two-course treatment sub-sequence.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or competency statements are present in the data, so a prospective student cannot see what the certificate certifies beyond the course titles.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish three or four competency outcomes (e.g., interpret environmental analytical data, evaluate remediation options, apply environmental regulations) for the certificate.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits is null and creditReconcileDelta is null, which for a graduate certificate is a transparency note rather than a defect; every listed course is 3 credits, implying a conventional ~4-course/12-credit credential. The focus is appropriate but the required credit count and number of courses are not stated.
dimensions.suggestion: State the total credits required (e.g., 12) and how many of the 17 listed courses a student must complete to earn the certificate.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Most course descriptions are present, specific, and informative (e.g., ENE 664, ENE 672 Stormwater Management, EVSC 610), but two courses, ENE 630 Physical Processes of Environmental Systems and ENE 661 Environmental Microbiology, have blank descriptions, and there is no plan of study or stated credit total.
dimensions.suggestion: Fill in the missing descriptions for ENE 630 and ENE 661 and add a short credit/course-count summary at the top of the certificate page.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The course set reflects current environmental practice well: site remediation (ENE 662), stormwater BMPs (ENE 672), toxicology and risk (EVSC 616), environmental microbiology (EVSC 627), and energy/sustainability (EVSC 715). It is comparatively light on geospatial analytics and air-quality/climate content, both now central to the field.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a GIS / environmental-data-analytics option (the parent M.S. already offers CE 602 Geographic Information System) so graduates can work with spatial monitoring data.

descriptionQuality.justification: The majority of the 17 descriptions are substantive and specific (ENE 664 on coagulation/flocculation/filtration, EVSC 610 on pollutant transport and decomposition), but ENE 630 and ENE 661 have entirely empty description excerpts, dragging down an otherwise informative set.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated as explicit course codes with no dangling references (missingCourseCount 0), e.g., ENE 662 requires EM 631 and ENE 664 requires ENE 663; clarity is only slightly reduced by CE 647 and EVSC 627 pointing to undergraduate prerequisites described in the undergraduate catalog.

missingTopics.rationale: The menu is water-, waste-, and soil-heavy (ENE 664/665, CE 647, ENE 662) with no dedicated air-pollution or emissions-control course.
Quantitative spatial analysis underpins modern environmental monitoring, yet no GIS course appears in this certificate menu even though the department offers CE 602.
Only EVSC 715 Energy and Sustainability touches energy; there is no course on carbon accounting or climate mitigation, now core to environmental practice.
The treatment and remediation courses (ENE 664/665, ENE 662) are framed around conventional pollutants and do not address the PFAS/emerging-contaminant problems dominating current regulation.
Policy coverage is legal and political (EM 631, EPS 622) with no explicit equity or community-engagement content that agencies now expect.

emergingTrends.rationale: Sensor networks and ML-driven analytics are transforming water and air monitoring; an analytics elective would extend EVSC 612 Environmental Analysis.
Low-impact development is displacing gray infrastructure; ENE 672 Stormwater Management could be modernized toward green-infrastructure design.
LCA is now standard for sustainability work; EVSC 715 could be paired with the department's ENE 673 Sustainability and Life Cycle Analysis.
Regulatory focus on PFAS makes ENE 662 Site Remediation a natural place to add advanced treatment and destruction technologies.
Energy transition is reshaping environmental engineering; EVSC 715 Energy and Sustainability could expand toward grid, storage, and renewables.

topSuggestions: State the required credit total and number of courses so the certificate's scope is transparent (statedTotalCredits is currently null).
Designate a required core within the 17-course menu so the credential has a defined spine rather than an eligible-course list.
Fill in the missing course descriptions for ENE 630 and ENE 661.
Add a GIS / environmental-data-analytics option (e.g., CE 602) to close the geospatial gap.
Publish three to four competency-based learning outcomes for the certificate.
